summary: 9 items surfaced | 21/21 stories covered | history + run artifact updated

items.name: Britannica11.org
items.source_story_title: Britannica11.org – a structured edition of the 1911 Encyclopædia Britannica
items.summary: A structured, searchable reconstruction of the 1911 Encyclopædia Britannica.
items.why_included: HN liked the reconstruction work and the site’s usefulness as a reference.
items.evidence: Commenters praised the 37k-article reconstruction, clickable sections, linked scans, and search; they also asked for easier scan/text comparison.

items.name: Framework Laptop 13 Pro
items.summary: A modular 13-inch laptop with deep backward compatibility and upgradeable parts.
items.why_included: HN was unusually enthusiastic about the compatibility story and repairability.
items.evidence: Comments highlighted hot-swappable upgrades into older Framework 13 machines and debated battery life, Linux support, and pricing.

items.name: GoModel
items.source_story_title: Show HN: GoModel – an open-source AI gateway in Go
items.summary: An open-source Go AI gateway for model routing, usage tracking, and caching.
items.why_included: HN saw it as a compact alternative amid LiteLLM supply-chain worries.
items.evidence: The thread discussed its tiny Docker image, OpenAI-compatible API, and comparisons to LiteLLM and other Go-based gateways.

items.name: VidStudio
items.source_story_title: Show HN: VidStudio, a browser based video editor that doesn't upload your files
items.summary: A local-first browser video editor that keeps projects on your machine.
items.why_included: HN liked the privacy angle and the fully client-side pipeline.
items.evidence: Comments praised no-upload, no-account editing while debating FFmpeg WASM licensing and the browser-only architecture.

items.name: MNT Reform
items.source_story_title: MNT Reform is an open hardware laptop, designed and assembled in Germany
items.summary: An open-hardware laptop built for repairability and long-term modification.
items.why_included: HN praised the full openness and the repair-first design philosophy.
items.evidence: Commenters highlighted source code, schematics, BOMs, modular parts, and compared its philosophy against Framework.

items.name: Ctx
items.source_story_title: Show HN: Ctx – a /resume that works across Claude Code and Codex
items.summary: A local SQLite-backed resume system that carries context across Claude Code and Codex.
items.why_included: HN was interested in cross-agent continuity and self-built harnesses.
items.evidence: Discussion focused on resuming workstreams between providers, local storage, and the value of a custom harness.

items.name: Mediator.ai
items.source_story_title: Show HN: Mediator.ai – Using Nash bargaining and LLMs to systematize fairness
items.summary: An LLM-assisted negotiation tool that searches for agreements using Nash bargaining.
items.why_included: HN engaged with the novelty of applying LLMs to mediation.
items.evidence: Comments debated whether it was mediation or arbitration, while the author explained it generates and scores candidate agreements.

items.name: Daemons
items.source_story_title: Show HN: Daemons – we pivoted from building agents to cleaning up after them
items.summary: Repository-local background routines that clean up drift from agent-created code.
items.why_included: HN liked the repo-local maintenance angle for agent-heavy workflows.
items.evidence: The thread compared it with Claude hooks and routines and focused on keeping PRs mergeable, docs current, and dependencies clean.

items.name: Fusion Power Plant Simulator
items.summary: A simulator for exploring fusion plant design, power recirculation, and economics.
items.why_included: HN found it useful for understanding the engineering and economic tradeoffs of fusion.
items.evidence: Comments praised the recirculating-power model and suggested adding plant cost, electricity price, and more realistic economics.
